label: Uneasy calm
summary:
The world enters the weekend caught between dread and progress. War still dominates: the rickety U.S.–Iran ceasefire holds only on paper as American casualties climb past 420 and gas prices sit at four-year highs, while Israel orders its army to seize most of Gaza and bombs Beirut. Yet diplomacy flickers — Washington and Tehran have reached a tentative memorandum to reopen the Strait of Hormuz and open 60-day nuclear talks, and oil is falling on the hope it sticks.

The planet itself is sending warnings. A new report projects global temperatures will breach the 1.5°C Paris limit, the UN says the hottest year on record is all but certain by 2030, and deadly heat has already killed dozens across India and Pakistan. An Ebola outbreak is spreading in eastern Congo.

But the day is not all gloom. Markets are buoyant — Japan's Nikkei topped 65,000 for the first time — Algeria became the latest nation to eliminate blinding trachoma, and a run of scientific breakthroughs in light-based AI, space computing and medical diagnosis hint at a more capable, healthier future. The mood is watchful: real danger, real hope, no resolution yet.

drivers.headline: US and Iran reach tentative agreement to reopen Strait of Hormuz and begin 60-day nuclear talks

Quad nations agree to jointly build a Fiji port and launch critical-minerals and energy-security initiatives
